cataloging_subject.label: The Canterbury Tales (Ellesmere/Furnivall representation)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's unfinished Middle English pilgrimage frame and twenty-four-tale collection as transmitted principally in Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 (the Ellesmere Chaucer) and represented by Frederick J. Furnivall's one-manuscript Chaucer Society edition. The cataloged unit includes the edition's visible witness-specific and editorial apparatus but does not count the non-Ellesmere Gamelyn and links, rejected Clerk link, illustration suites, or correction leaf as Chaucerian tales. The physical manuscript, printed edition, digital facsimiles, U-M encoding, and repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer; anonymous or uncertain responsibility for non-Ellesmere appendix texts
catalog_summary.date: Composed chiefly in the late 1380s and 1390s; Ellesmere manuscript c. 1400-1410; source edition issued 1868-1884
catalog_summary.region: England; associated with late-medieval London literary language

notes: The qualifying Ellesmere/Furnivall wording belongs to the cataloging-unit label, not to the conventional title of Chaucer's work.

titles.value: The Ellesmere MS. of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
notes: Title of the immediate printed and electronic transmission layer.

scope.description: The Ellesmere-ordered Canterbury Tales core, including the Retraction; excludes separately sourced appendix texts and visual/editorial apparatus

notes: The rejected seven-line Clerk link remains inside the core edition group as an explicitly marked editorially rejected passage, not an independently counted tale.

scope.description: The 1868-1884 one-manuscript edition and its appended materials, not authorship of the medieval work
Transcription and editorial presentation of the Ellesmere-based source edition, with separately identified appendix witnesses

date_statements.display: Begun probably in the late 1380s and worked on through the 1390s; unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame; the date does not apply automatically to anonymous appendix texts.

scope.description: The ten-fragment Ellesmere-ordered Canterbury Tales core, not the non-Ellesmere appendix or modern edition apparatus and not necessarily the earliest attestation of every tale

notes: This is a witness-production date, not a composition date.

date_statements.display: Chaucer Society edition issued in eight parts, 1868-1884; final title page dated 1868-1879

scope.description: The exact edition-defined content and apparatus represented by agz8232

places.label: Southwark
notes: Narrative departure point, not asserted as the place of composition.

scope.description: Literary-language association for the ten-fragment Canterbury Tales core

notes: A literary-language association, not a securely documented composition site and not a claim about the separately sourced appendix or modern apparatus.

scope.description: Departure point in the pilgrimage frame established by the General Prologue
Destination in the pilgrimage frame established by the General Prologue

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin rubrics and quotations do not displace Middle English as the primary language; appendix spellings reflect their separate witnesses.

form_statements.description: Predominantly verse, with the prose Tale of Melibee and Parson's Tale; the Retraction is prose apparatus and is not counted as a third tale.

scope.description: The pilgrimage frame introduced in the General Prologue
Especially the General Prologue's social portraits
A constituent mode, serious and parodic
Constituent fabliaux and closely related tale modes

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: A company of pilgrims traveling from Southwark toward Thomas Becket's shrine at Canterbury agree to tell stories. Chaucer's unfinished frame juxtaposes social portraits, competing voices, literary genres, moral argument, and performances ranging from romance, saint's life, and Marian miracle to fabliau, beast fable, prose instruction, and penitential sermon. This representation preserves the distinctive Ellesmere fragment order and Furnivall's edition-level apparatus.

The complete inventory has seventy-five parts: ten fragment/group containers; fifty-five core components containing exactly twenty-four counted tales; one appendix container with five non-counted appendix components; and one visual/correction container with three non-counted apparatus components. The appendix records Gamelyn and its link from Royal 18 C II, the Man of Law-Shipman link from Arch. Selden B.14, the Nun's Priest end-link from Dd.4.24, and drawings from Gg.4.27. The visual/correction layer records twenty-three Hooper teller cuts, twenty-three manuscript-miniature facsimiles, and Flügel's 1907 corrections.

This record catalogs Chaucer's composite work through one specific witness-and-edition representation. The medieval manuscript, Furnivall edition, U-M encoding and page images, repository XML, and third-party scans are separate lineage entities. Apparatus is retained for faithful discovery without silently treating Gamelyn, supplementary links, drawings, facsimile plates, or corrections as Chaucerian tales.

extent.description: Exactly twenty-four tales are counted: twenty-two verse tales and two prose tales. The seventy-five-part inventory also exposes all containers, links, prologues, interruptions, the prose Retraction, appendix texts, drawings, facsimiles, and correction apparatus. The XML contains 784 LG and 18,309 L elements in total. Excluding four appendix LG with 949 L and the rejected Clerk-link LG with seven L yields 779 verse groups and 17,353 verse-line elements in the Ellesmere-ordered core; these are encoding-derived metrics, not a new critical lineation.

content_parts.label: Group A, Fragment I
content_parts.summary: General Prologue and the Knight, Miller, Reeve, and incomplete Cook sequence.

content_parts.label: General Prologue
content_parts.summary: Pilgrimage frame, pilgrim portraits, and tale-telling agreement.

content_parts.label: The Knight's Tale
content_parts.summary: The Knight's verse romance of Palamon, Arcite, and Emelye.

content_parts.label: Miller's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Miller interrupts the planned social order of telling.

content_parts.label: The Miller's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of Nicholas, Alisoun, Absolon, and John.

content_parts.label: Reeve's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Reeve answers the Miller and introduces his retaliatory tale.

content_parts.label: The Reeve's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of the miller Symkyn and two Cambridge clerks.

content_parts.label: Cook's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Cook reacts to the Reeve's tale and begins his own.

content_parts.label: The Cook's Tale
content_parts.summary: Incomplete fabliau of Perkyn Revel.
notes: The separately sourced Tale of Gamelyn is retained only in the appendix and is not used to complete this tale.

content_parts.label: Group B-alpha, Fragment II
content_parts.summary: Man of Law head-link, prologue, and tale.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's Head-link
content_parts.summary: Host's timekeeping and summons to the Man of Law.

content_parts.label: Man of Law's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Man of Law introduces his tale and comments on Chaucer's writings.

content_parts.label: The Man of Law's Tale
content_parts.summary: Verse tale of Constance's exile, trials, and restoration.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, and Summoner sequence.

content_parts.label: Wife of Bath's Preamble
content_parts.summary: The Wife's autobiographical and argumentative prologue.

content_parts.label: Friar-Summoner Interruption
content_parts.summary: The Friar and Summoner interrupt the Wife's preamble and quarrel.

content_parts.label: The Wife of Bath's Tale
content_parts.summary: Arthurian romance of a knight learning what women most desire.

content_parts.label: Friar's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Friar prepares a satirical tale against summoners.

content_parts.label: The Friar's Tale
content_parts.summary: A corrupt summoner encounters a devil.

content_parts.label: Summoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Summoner answers the Friar with an anti-fraternal preface.

content_parts.label: The Summoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau and clerical satire of Friar John and Thomas.

content_parts.label: Summoner's Tale Fart-distribution Coda
content_parts.summary: The squire's proposed geometric division of Thomas's gift.

content_parts.label: Group E, Fragment VI
content_parts.summary: Clerk and Merchant sequence; the Squire prologue begins at the group's end and continues in Group F.

content_parts.label: Clerk's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls upon the Clerk to tell.

content_parts.label: The Clerk's Tale
content_parts.summary: The Clerk's six-part tale of Griselda, followed by Chaucer's envoy.

content_parts.label: Rejected Clerk End-link
content_parts.summary: Seven-line link printed by Furnivall as possibly original but rejected apparatus.
notes: Excluded from the 17,353-line core metric and from counted tales.

content_parts.label: Merchant's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Merchant reacts to the Clerk and introduces his own marital tale.

content_parts.label: The Merchant's Tale
content_parts.summary: January, May, and Damyan in a marriage tale with fabliau elements.

content_parts.label: Group F, Fragment VII
content_parts.summary: Squire and Franklin sequence.

content_parts.label: Squire's Head-link and Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host turns to the Squire; Furnivall's encoding splits the continuous introductory unit across the end of Group E and start of Group F.

content_parts.label: The Squire's Tale
content_parts.summary: Incomplete romance of Cambyuskan and marvelous gifts.

content_parts.label: Squire-Franklin Link
content_parts.summary: The Franklin praises the Squire and the Host calls on the Franklin.

content_parts.label: Franklin's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Franklin disclaims rhetorical learning before his Breton lay.

content_parts.label: The Franklin's Tale
content_parts.summary: Breton lay of Dorigen, Arveragus, Aurelius, and competing promises.

content_parts.label: Group C, Fragment IV
content_parts.summary: Physician and Pardoner sequence.

content_parts.label: The Physician's Tale
content_parts.summary: Moral tragedy of Virginia and Apius.

content_parts.label: Physician-Pardoner Link
content_parts.summary: The Host responds to the Physician's tale and calls on the Pardoner.

content_parts.label: Pardoner's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Pardoner exposes his fraudulent preaching practice.

content_parts.label: The Pardoner's Tale
content_parts.summary: Exemplum of three rioters who seek Death and destroy one another.

content_parts.label: Group B-beta, Fragment III
content_parts.summary: Shipman through Nun's Priest sequence in the Ellesmere/Furnivall order.

content_parts.label: The Shipman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Fabliau of a merchant, his wife, and the monk Daun John.

content_parts.label: Shipman-Prioress Link
content_parts.summary: The Host's words after the Shipman's tale and summons to the Prioress.

content_parts.label: Prioress's Prologue
content_parts.summary: Marian invocation before the Prioress's miracle tale.

content_parts.label: The Prioress's Tale
content_parts.summary: Miracle of the Virgin centered on a murdered schoolboy; contains antisemitic narrative material.

content_parts.label: Host-Chaucer Link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls on Chaucer the pilgrim to tell.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: Interrupted parody of tail-rhyme romance told by Chaucer the pilgrim.

content_parts.label: Host's Interruption of Sir Thopas
content_parts.summary: The Host stops Sir Thopas and requests a different tale.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Melibee
content_parts.summary: Prose tale and debate on counsel, vengeance, and reconciliation.

content_parts.label: Monk's Prologue
content_parts.summary: The Host's words to the Monk and the Monk's definition of tragedy.

content_parts.label: The Monk's Tale
content_parts.summary: Series of eighteen tragedies of falls from prosperity.
notes: The eighteen internal tragedy divisions are subdivisions of one conventional pilgrim tale, not eighteen counted tales.

content_parts.label: Monk-Nun's Priest Link and Knight's Interruption
content_parts.summary: The Knight stops the Monk; the Host calls on the Nun's Priest.

content_parts.label: The Nun's Priest's Tale
content_parts.summary: Beast fable of Chauntecleer, Pertelote, and the fox.

content_parts.label: Group G, Fragment VIII
content_parts.summary: Second Nun and Canon's Yeoman sequence.

content_parts.label: Second Nun's Prologue
content_parts.summary: Invocation and etymological praise of Saint Cecilia.

content_parts.label: The Second Nun's Tale
content_parts.summary: Saint's life of Cecilia.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman Preamble
content_parts.summary: Arrival and departure of the Canon and the Yeoman's introductory exposure of alchemy.

content_parts.label: The Canon's Yeoman's Tale
content_parts.summary: Alchemy tale exposing a fraudulent canon.

content_parts.label: Group H, Fragment IX
content_parts.summary: Manciple head-link and tale.

content_parts.label: Manciple's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host's exchange with the Cook and call for the Manciple's tale.

content_parts.label: The Manciple's Tale
content_parts.summary: Moral tale of Phoebus and the crow.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Parson head-link, Furnivall's analysis, prose tale, and Retraction.

content_parts.label: Parson's Head-link
content_parts.summary: The Host calls for the final tale and the Parson promises moral prose.

content_parts.label: Contents and Analysis of the Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Furnivall's omitted-from-running-text analytical contents preceding the tale.

locators.value: pp. 587, 587a, 587b, 588, and 588a; Parson text begins at p. 588b

content_parts.label: The Parson's Tale
content_parts.summary: Prose penitential treatise through the three parts of penitence.

content_parts.label: Retraction
content_parts.summary: The closing prose retraction beginning with the maker taking leave.
notes: A distinct epilogue, not a third prose tale.

content_parts.label: Furnivall Appendix Texts and Drawings
content_parts.summary: Separately sourced texts and drawings printed with the edition but not counted as Canterbury Tales core items.

content_parts.label: Spurious Gamelyn Link
content_parts.summary: Two-line link transcribed from British Library Royal MS 18 C II.
notes: Not present in Ellesmere and not counted as a tale.

content_parts.label: The Tale of Gamelyn
content_parts.summary: Nine-hundred-three-line non-Ellesmere tale transcribed from Royal MS 18 C II.
notes: Retained as appendix apparatus and excluded from the conventional count of twenty-four Chaucerian tales.

content_parts.label: Man of Law-Shipman Link
content_parts.summary: Twenty-eight-line link from Bodleian Library MS Arch. Selden B.14.

content_parts.label: Nun's Priest's End-link
content_parts.summary: Sixteen-line extract from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.4.24, fol. 195b.

content_parts.label: Cambridge Gg.4.27 Drawing Appendix
content_parts.summary: Drawings of the Cook, Reeve, Wife, Pardoner, Monk, and Manciple, with six allegorical figures, reproduced from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27.

content_parts.label: Visual and Correction Apparatus
content_parts.summary: Edition-level teller drawings, manuscript miniature facsimiles, and the later correction leaf.

content_parts.label: Hooper Drawings of the 23 Tellers
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three drawings copied from the Ellesmere miniatures and cut on wood by W. H. Hooper.

content_parts.label: Facsimiles of the 23 Ellesmere Miniatures
content_parts.summary: Separate suite of twenty-three facsimiles of the manuscript's teller miniatures.

content_parts.label: Ewald Flügel's 1907 Corrections
content_parts.summary: Correction leaf for the Six-Text and separate Ellesmere print.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML for agz8232.
evidence.locator: HEADER; TEXT/BODY/DIV1; TEXT/BACK/DIV1-DIV3; PB, MILESTONE, LG, L, and P elements
evidence.summary: Exact repository source for the U-M identifier, Furnivall source statement, Ellesmere group order, core and appendix divisions, omission placeholders, pagination, and encoded text.

evidence.citation: XPath and sequence audit of CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/agz8232.xml.
evidence.locator: All DIV1-DIV3, HEAD, LG, L, P, PB, MILESTONE, N, NODE, and TYPE values
evidence.summary: Finds ten core group containers, fifty-five core components, a five-component appendix, and three visual/correction apparatus components. Counts 784 LG and 18,309 L overall; subtracting four appendix LG/949 L and one rejected Clerk-link LG/seven L yields the 779 LG and 17,353 L core metrics. Confirms 846 unique PB values in the exact sequence 1-846 and the edition's represented and omitted ranges.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, AGZ8232.0001.001, The Ellesmere ms. of Chaucer's Canterbury tales.
evidence.summary: Official item record and table of contents confirming the current public item, Chaucer and Furnivall responsibilities, edition citation, group sequence, page/text views, and No Copyright-United States designation.

evidence.citation: Companion lineage record for Furnivall's 1868-1884 Ellesmere edition, its complete bibliographic extent, witness sources, carrier audits, illustration suites, appendices, and 1907 correction leaf.
evidence.summary: Supplies the edition-layer evidence used to delimit the 75-part representation and to keep core text, non-Ellesmere appendix texts, drawings, manuscript facsimiles, and corrections separate.

evidence.citation: Companion lineage record for Huntington Library MS EL 26 C 9 and its official catalog and IIIF evidence.
evidence.summary: Supports the Ellesmere witness identity, c. 1400-1410 date, physical extent, contents, miniature program, and relationship to Furnivall's edition.

evidence.citation: Helen Cooper, The Canterbury Tales, 3rd ed., Oxford Guides to Chaucer, chapter 1, The Canterbury Tales (Oxford University Press, 2023).
evidence.summary: Current scholarly synthesis for the probable late-1380s beginning, continued work through the 1390s and perhaps to 1400, and uncertainty of individual tale dates.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, Fragments or Groups of Tales.
evidence.summary: Explains the ten internally linked fragments, unfinished state, and absence of a securely authorial final order.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, Middle English Dialects.
evidence.summary: Supports a London literary-language association without treating edited spelling as transparent evidence of a whole-work composition place.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, The General Prologue.
evidence.summary: Supports the Southwark-to-Canterbury pilgrimage frame and the General Prologue's estates-satire and social-portrait functions.
Identifies the Miller, Reeve, Shipman, Summoner, and fragmentary Cook tales as fabliaux and notes fabliau affinities elsewhere.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, The Prioress's Tale.
evidence.summary: Supports miracle-of-the-Virgin classification and contextualizes the tale's antisemitic narrative.

evidence.citation: Harvard's Geoffrey Chaucer Website, The Second Nun's Tale.
evidence.summary: Identifies the tale as a saint's life of Cecilia.

open_questions.question: What order, if any, did Chaucer intend for the ten fragments?
next_steps: Treat the Ellesmere/Furnivall sequence as a witness and editorial arrangement, not an authorial fact.
Preserve letter-group and fragment crosswalks in discovery displays.

open_questions.question: What authorship and textual status should be assigned independently to Gamelyn and the three supplementary links?
next_steps: Keep all five appendix components at item_count 0 in this representation.
Create independent work records only after component-specific textual research.

open_questions.question: Should the twenty-four conventional tales receive independent work records?
next_steps: Retain the complete nested inventory here.
Avoid inheriting whole-collection claims when creating any future tale-level records.

notes: The twenty-four-tale count is semantic: prologues, links, interruptions, Retraction, apparatus, and the non-Ellesmere Tale of Gamelyn do not increment it.
Group letters and fragment numerals follow the Furnivall/Ellesmere presentation; their sequence is witness-specific and editorially mediated.
The Parson's Tale and Melibee are prose even where digital serialization uses line-like structures; visual apparatus uses textual_form other.
No reusable modeling problem beyond the project's current boundary and rights case lessons was discovered, so no shared skill file is changed on this research branch.
